top_winning_behaviors.insight: Reps who establish a concrete next action (calendar invite, time commitment) book at 96% vs 59%, a +37pt delta—this locks commitment and prevents ghosting.
top_winning_behaviors.evidence_stat: 9.6 score booked vs 5.9 not booked, +3.7 delta

top_winning_behaviors.insight: Reps who invest in connection (humor, empathy, personal interest) score 6.9 vs 5.1, a +1.8 delta—restaurant owners respond to relationship before product in trust-based industries.
top_winning_behaviors.evidence_stat: 6.9 rapport score booked vs 5.1 not booked

top_winning_behaviors.insight: Reps who engage objections (acknowledge, reframe, offer proof) score 5.6 vs 4.6, a +1.0 delta—overcoming resistance signals value and earns the demo slot.
top_winning_behaviors.evidence_stat: 5.6 objection handling score booked vs 4.6 not booked

top_objections.best_response: Acknowledge urgency, micro-commit to 15-min demo at specific future time, frame as 'quick audit' not sales call
top_objections.example_rep_behavior: Totally get it—15 minutes Thursday at 2pm to show you one thing that saves you $800/month, then you decide. Sound fair?

top_objections.best_response: Validate their choice, shift to 'supplement not replace' positioning, use social proof of restaurants using both
top_objections.example_rep_behavior: Love that you're on it—most of our customers keep DoorDash for new customers but use us for repeat orders where margins matter. 15 min to see if it fits?

top_objections.best_response: Ask what changed or didn't resonate, introduce new feature/proof point, reframe as 'update call' not cold pitch
top_objections.example_rep_behavior: Makes sense—what wasn't a fit last time? We just added [feature], and three restaurants in [their area] are seeing [result]. Worth a fresh look?

opening_angle_analysis.insight: Following up on an inbound signal (even cold) creates implied permission and reciprocity—owner feels they initiated, reducing cold-call resistance and triggering consistency bias.
opening_angle_analysis.worst_insight: Generic product-first pitch signals 'vendor spam'—owners hear this daily from 10+ platforms, triggers automatic rejection before value is established.

tenure_insight: Mid-tenure reps book 32% (20/63), new reps 29% (10/35), senior reps 39% (19/49). Senior reps show a +7-10pt edge, but the delta doesn't meet the 15pt significance bar with this sample size. The pattern suggests experience helps but isn't determinative—likely because restaurant calling rewards empathy and hustle over complex enterprise sales skills. More data needed to confirm if senior reps' advantage scales or if mid/new reps can match performance with better scripts and coaching on rapport + next-step clarity.
duration_insight: Booked calls average 6.5 minutes vs 3.9 for not-booked—a 2.6-minute delta. This likely reflects both causality directions: (1) interested owners stay on the line longer, AND (2) reps who invest time in discovery, rapport, and objection handling create interest. The 3.9-minute not-booked average suggests reps are getting past gatekeepers and into conversations, but failing to hook the owner in the first 90 seconds. Implication: coach reps to frontload value and social proof in the first 60 seconds, then earn the next 3-5 minutes for discovery and close.

three_things_we_didnt_know.title: Demo Followup Beats Product Pitches 7:1
three_things_we_didnt_know.detail: Demo_request_followup converts at 63% (31/49 booked) vs online_ordering at 9% (2/22) and fee_savings at 15% (4/26)—a 48-54pt gap. Mechanism: framing the call as a followup (even if cold) triggers reciprocity and lowers defenses, while product-first pitches signal spam. Reps should open every call with 'following up on your interest in…' or 'saw you checked out our site' to manufacture permission. Managers: rewrite all cold scripts to mimic warm followup language.

three_things_we_didnt_know.title: Talk Ratio Is the Team's Systemic Blind Spot
three_things_we_didnt_know.detail: talk_ratio_score is the weakest signal for 8 of 15 reps — more than any other behavior. Booked calls average a 7.1 talk ratio score vs 5.9 for not-booked calls, a 1.2 point delta. This isn't a rep-by-rep coaching problem — it's a systemic habit the whole team has developed. Reps are pitching when they should be listening. The fix is structural: add a talk ratio tracker to the weekly coaching cadence and set a team target of 40-55% rep talking per call.

three_things_we_didnt_know.title: 61% Not-Booked Show Followup Interest—Auto-Sequence
three_things_we_didnt_know.detail: 60 of 98 not-booked calls (61%) showed followup interest signals but didn't convert on first call. Current process loses them. High-behavior not-booked (22 calls, score ≥7) cite timing, existing vendor satisfaction, or setup chaos—not product rejection. Implication: build automated 3-5 touch email/SMS sequence for 'interested but not now' bucket. Sales ops should tag these leads for 30/60/90-day re-engagement, capturing 15-25 incremental demos/quarter without additional dial volume.

segment_angle_insights.insight: Indian restaurants convert at 4x overall rate (80% vs 20% baseline)—likely newer to direct ordering tech, less vendor fatigue, higher trust in relationship-first outreach.
Small sample (3 calls) but perfect conversion—Southern owners may value personal outreach and local business storytelling; test fee_savings angle next to confirm.
Demo followup works (6/8 booked) but fee_savings underperforms (33%, 2/6)—suggests Mexican owners respond to relationship-building over ROI pitches; avoid leading with cost savings.
American cuisine shows balanced response to multiple angles (50% for fee/Google, 62% demo followup)—most versatile segment, test all angles to optimize rep flexibility.

Italian drastically underperforms across all angles (11-25%)—possible ICP mismatch, higher tech saturation, or stronger existing vendor relationships; consider deprioritizing or testing Spanish-language reps.

high_behavior_not_booked.pattern: These calls show high rapport, discovery, and objection handling (score ≥7) but cite timing, vendor satisfaction, or operational chaos ('we just signed with X', 'in the middle of buyout', 'need to set up other things first')—not product rejection.
high_behavior_not_booked.gtm_implication: This is a timing/sequencing problem, not product-market fit. 22 high-quality conversations lost to pipeline leakage. GTM should build a 'high-intent nurture' track—tag these leads for automated 60-day re-engagement when timing improves. Sales ops: create CRM status 'Good Fit—Bad Timing' to preserve context and trigger future outreach.

follow_up_opportunity.implication: 61% of not-booked calls showed interest signals but didn't convert immediately—this is 60 qualified leads/147 calls (41% of total volume) leaking from pipeline. A 3-5 touch email/SMS sequence ('Hey [Owner], still thinking about cutting DoorDash fees? Here's a 2-min video...') could recapture 15-20% (9-12 demos), adding 18-24% to monthly bookings without increasing dial volume. Sales ops should auto-enroll these contacts and track conversion to prove ROI of nurture infrastructure.

behavior_score_methodology.description: Composite of 7 signals. Weights = domain knowledge; calibrate via regression at 1000+ calls.
